  • Internals and Porting Notes
    • Available internals pages
  • diCal2 Native Parity Notes
    • Why these changes were necessary
    • What changed
      • 1. Shared upstream/native result normalization
      • 2. VCF reader now preserves upstream-style physical-locus metadata
      • 3. Grouped-locus counting now uses physical blocks, not just segregating columns
      • 4. Refined intervals now materialize interval-specific epochs
      • 5. Meta-start generation was tightened toward upstream semantics
      • 6. Gallery rerun used a targeted diCal2-only render path
      • 7. Meta-start replay tracing is now available on the native path
    • What improved
    • What still does not match
    • Next steps
  • PSMC Internals
    • Why this page exists
    • Source map
    • Core model
    • The parts that matter for parity
    • What stayed out of the main docs

    • Core ingredients

    • Handoff notes
    • Upstream bridge notes
    • Where to read code
    • What stayed out of the main docs
  • SMC++ Parity Closure Notes
    • Scope
    • Final state
    • What was actually wrong
      • 1. Native one-pop preprocessing was not phase-aligned with upstream
      • 2. Native one-pop HMM was leaving the upstream compressed-row ceremony
    • How the issue was found
    • The fixes that closed parity
      • Upstream preprocessing offset in native one-pop preprocessing
      • Stay on the compressed run-length stream in the one-pop HMM
    • Earlier fixes that mattered
      • One-pop Watterson estimator
      • Monomorphic one-pop emission handling
      • One-pop transition details
      • Prefit / initialization parity
      • Scale-step optimizer semantics
    • What did not close parity by itself
    • Practical lesson
    • Code pointers

smckit
  • Quickstart: diCal2
  • View page source

Quickstart: diCal2

This quickstart uses a small README-style diCal2 example bundle that ships with the Python package.

What the input files are

diCal2 works from a small file bundle rather than one file:

  • .param for mutation/recombination parameters

  • .demo for the demographic model

  • .config for sample assignments

  • sometimes .rates

  • sequence data, often VCF plus a reference

Packaged examples:

  • smckit.io.example_path("dical2/test.param")

  • smckit.io.example_path("dical2/exp.demo")

  • smckit.io.example_path("dical2/exp.config")

  • smckit.io.example_path("dical2/test.vcf")

Run diCal2

import smckit

param_file = smckit.io.example_path("dical2/test.param")
demo_file = smckit.io.example_path("dical2/exp.demo")
config_file = smckit.io.example_path("dical2/exp.config")
sequence_file = smckit.io.example_path("dical2/test.vcf")
reference_file = smckit.io.example_path("dical2/test.fa")

data = smckit.io.read_dical2(
    sequences=sequence_file,
    param_file=param_file,
    demo_file=demo_file,
    config_file=config_file,
    reference_file=reference_file,
    filter_pass_string=".",
)

data = smckit.tl.dical2(
    data,
    n_intervals=11,
    max_t=4.0,
    n_em_iterations=2,
    composite_mode="pac",
    implementation="native",
)

The important choices here are composite_mode="pac" and keeping the example off the advanced meta-start options. This is the cleanest first run before you start changing bounds, start points, or multi-start search settings.

Inspect the result

res = data.results["dical2"]

print(res["implementation"])
print(res["log_likelihood"])
print(res["pop_sizes"])

For a fuller explanation of the file bundle, start-point controls, and result fields, see the diCal2 method page.

Next steps

  • diCal2 method page

  • I/O formats

  • Parity notes
